title: 2025/12/11 - FD
displayTitle: 2025-12-11 - Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee - International treaty examination of the Agreement to Establish the Pacific Resilience Facility
committee: Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee
topic: International treaty examination of the Agreement to Establish the Pacific Resilience Facility
description: Thursday 11 December 2025 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee International treaty examination of the Agreement to Establish the Pacific Resilience Facility Hearing of evidence Submitters List: Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade Hayden Everett, Unit Manager, Governance & Economics, Development Economy, and Prosperity Division Christine Nam, Lead Advisor, General International Law, Legal Division Sophia Lourie, Policy Officer, Governance & Economics, Development Economy, and Prosperity Division
descriptionSummary: International treaty examination of the Agreement to Establish the Pacific Resilience Facility. Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade. Hayden Everett, Unit Manager, Governance & Economics, Development Economy, and Prosperity Division

chunks.text: Sorry, their time- Anyway, it should be-... their time hasn't been updated. Should be 20 minutes. So, if, if you could remind them, sorry, the agenda's out of date. Um, it should... They've got t- uh- Yeah, 20 minutes, wasn't it? 20 minutes for the hearing and then five minutes for consideration. Sorry Yeah. Okay. All right. We'll bring them in. Thank you. Uh... Uh...
Oh,

is this the latest one? No, it's the same one. Uh... Oh, that might be the new one. Oh, yes, yes, this is the newest one, yeah. This is the new one. Okay. All right. Good afternoon. Welcome, welcome- Welcome.... to the committee today. Thank you.

Appreciate you coming in on relatively short notice to talk to the Pacific Resilience Facility Agreement. Obviously, we've received that international treaty just recently, uh, and the national interest analysis alongside that. Mm-hmm. Given the timing on these consideration pieces, we always like to move efficiently. So we have you in for 20 minutes today.

I'm sure committee will have questions, but we'll hand over to you to give some introductory comments and talk through that. Uh, you haven't got a written submission for us, is that correct? Uh, that will flow later, yes. Okay. All right. Over to you. Thank you for the opportunity to talk to the Pacific Resilience Facility Establishment Agreement today. Uh, my name is Hayden Everett from the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, and I'm here with my colleagues, uh, Christine Lam and Sofia Varu.

The Pacific Resilience Facility Establishment Agreement will establish the PRF as a multilateral treaty level organisation- (clears throat)... uh, to be domiciled in Tonga. The PRF will utilize investment returns generated from a capital base to make grants in support of climate and disaster resilience in the Pacific. Uh, grants will support projects that are required to, uh, build resilience and reduce the vulnerability and exposure of Pacific communities to disaster risks from climate change and other hazards.

It'll be the first Pacific-owned and -led regional financing facility for climate and disaster resilience. And, uh, Pacific leaders have identified this as the appropriate mechanism for supporting their, uh, climate and disaster resilience ambitions. Uh, New Zealand has pledged $20 million, uh, towards the capitalisation of the PRF, and we signed the PRF Treaty along with 15 other Pacific countries, uh, in September '25 at the Pacific Island Forum Leaders Meeting.

Uh, the agreement will come into force once eight countries have ratified, uh, the agreement and deposited the instrument of ratification with the Pacific Islands Forum Secretariat. Uh, three countries have ratified the agreement so far, uh, Nauru, uh, Marshall Islands, and Tonga, the host country of the facility. Uh, so parties to the treaty will include all Pacific Island Forum members, uh, with the potential for associate members of the Pacific Islands Forum to so join. Uh, the, the PRF itself will be administered by, um, and overseen by a board

of directors. Uh, and then there'll be a, uh, chief executive and staff for the, the day-to-day operations of the facility. Uh, our support to the facility is an important part of our role as a responsive development partner to the Pacific. Um, Pacific countries have consistently identified climate change and, um, disaster resilience as one of the biggest challenges, if not the greatest challenge, that they face.

And their desire to establish a Pacific governed and managed facility has driven our, our support for, for becoming a member of the PRF and making a contribution. Uh, the agreement itself has no domestic, uh, operation in, in New Zealand. Um, as a, as a PRF council member, we'll be required to attend the, the annual council meetings. Uh, although they'll be serviced through our existing regional, um, meetings that MFAT, uh, attends. Uh, the Ministry for Regulation has confirmed that the agreement does not present

any, uh, domestic legislative change obligations and therefore does not trigger the requirement for a regulatory impact analysis. So in summary, um, uh, the, the, the ratification of this agreement is an important part o- of our contribution to the region's climate and disaster resilience goals. And ratification is important for our influence at the, the PRF Council, um, particularly to support a future application from, uh, Tokelau for membership to the facility. Um, Tokelau is a associate member of the Pacific Islands Forum, and the, um, establishment agreement

preserves a pathway for Tokelau to become a member of the facility. Um, by being one of the first countries to ratify the agreement, it allows us to, um, influence, um, the ability of Tokelau to become a, a member of the facility. Thank you.

Excellent. All right. Thank you for that summary, Hayden. Uh, do we have questions? Yes, the Honorable Ian Auger. (coughs) Mr. Chair, uh, thank you for that. Um, my only question is around, uh, the challenges through the PIF, uh, accepting that the PRF sits separate to the PIF.

Uh, we're satisfied that any...... any happenings or instabilities or otherwise in the, in the PIF won't affect either the membership moving forward or any of the functions and order processes 'cause I note in the, in the paperwork that I've been rereading- Hm.... uh, that part of it is a report back through the- Mm-hmm.

That's right. So, uh, at the moment the establishment of the PRF is, sits within the Pacific Islands Forum. So there's an establishment- Yeah.... unit there that, um- (clears throat)... is supporting, um, the establishment of the PRF but, uh, at, at a point in the not too distant future it will be transitioned over to Tonga and be governed by the direct- Rules-... rules and requirements and procedures that you see both in the establishment agreement but also a whole suite of, um, policies and guidance that is now being developed.

Um, so the establishment agreement was sort of the first overarching document and sitting with u- sitting under that is a series of operational policies which, um, ourselves and our Pacific countries are closely involved in designing. Um, and I think that will provide the, the protection and address some of the, the concerns.

But the avenue- So actually-... still comes through the PIF. That's right. Uh, correct, yep. Okay. Thank you. Loyal Trust. Thank you. (laughs) You named me. Sorry. (laughs) Mr. Jeffs. Laura McClure. Sorry, I changed my name. To McClure. Oh, okay. I've had that for a wee while. Oh, okay. I love it. Um, so I've just got like a couple of questions like around the finances. We-... are committing the 20 million but- Yep.

... have, what are the ongoing costs? Um, or forecasted costs of contributions towards that? Sure. So, uh, 20 million is our contribution to the initial- Mm-hmm.... uh, capital base. Um, we have not made a decision yet on- Yeah. ... moving forward, um, the future commitments. Um, the, I think, you know, we want to see this facility functioning and see how it works in terms of disbursement of grants in the Pacific before making any- Sure.

... um, further, further commitment. Okay. Um, there's no requirement to pay an ongoing, uh, membership fee to be, um, part of the- Mm-hmm.... part of the facility so our Pacific countries will pay, uh, a membership fee but that requirement does not apply to Australia and New Zealand because- Mm-hmm.

... we're not receiving grants from the, the facility. Okay. So, so some of the other island nations will be paying their membership in order to get the grants? Yep. So, in a way the facility's going to act like a grant distributor/hub for potential collaboration amongst the islands on some of these issues? That's right. So- In theory.

Yeah, so- Okay.... um, the target is, uh- Mm-hmm.... 500 million for the, the capital base. The-... um, capital base will be, uh, the intention is to largely preserve the, the capital base- Okay.... and use the interest for grants. Right. Uh, the interest will both support project, uh, projects in specific countries but also, uh, potentially regional projects.

Okay. Next question, Dana Kirkpatrick. Thank you. It kind of follows on from that. Um, I, I note that the, the 20 million is coming through the IDC program. That's correct. Um, and so I guess my qu- follow on question from that is how does this, uh, agreement and what might come from it in terms of projects or funding, um, sit alongside the IDC work that's already being done in the Pacific by New Zealand, um, and others of course, but, um, what's the crossover? How does it, how do they work in tandem?

What's the... Yeah. So, I think the key focus for this facility is projects at a community level. Uh, so these are going to be quite small, um, local projects in many cases. Um, it complements a lot of the other work we're doing through the, uh, IDC program in the, in the climate area as well as what others are doing. Um, the, some of the, the larger funders in this space, for example, the Green Climate Fund, Asian Development Bank, World Bank, uh, you know, their focus is on those larger, um, projects.

This, uh, facility fills a, sort of a niche which has been identified for community level, uh, resilience projects. Okay. Thank Yeah. Um- Sorry. Will there be any like financial reporting on like projects and, uh, like the transparency on who delivers those projects, et cetera?

Absolutely. So, um, that's what we're working, uh, through now in terms of the, uh, guidelines and- Mm-hmm.... policies which sit underneath this establishment agreement. Okay. Uh, so there's a requirement, um, for regular reporting- Correct.... uh, auditing, um, transparency as well as, uh, what you'd expect to see around environmental and social safeguards for- Mm-hmm.

... projects. Uh, so, um, you know, we're drawing from, um, some of those global facilities- Mm-hmm.... that, um, operate in this space to build a, a set of policies that are appropriate for a community level grants making facility. Hm. Okay.

I have a few questions. Uh, I might just pick up on one that Laura McClure made before- Yeah. Thank you, Mr. Chair. ... in relation to the on, ongoing costs for us. So obviously no commitment to an ongoing annual cost for us- Mm-hmm.... but from an estimate perspective- Mm-hmm.... resourcing, staff-wise, is that likely to be the sort of contribution we're making?

There'll be a, um, uh, staff time obviously in terms of, um, you know, monitoring our investment into the facility, monitoring, um, what the, uh-... the facility is doing, you know- And staff working within the facility? No, not staff w- That would be a contribution from us?

No, uh- That's interesting.... not staff working. So our only direct financial contribution at this point is the 20 million to capitalisation. Uh, there would obviously be, in fact, sort of staff time in terms of- Yeah, sure.... people receiving and reviewing reports from the facility. Okay. So you mentioned that, um, we aren't paying an annual fee because we've chosen not to receive any benefits from them.

Why have you made that decision? The facility is essentially designed to support, um, Pacific countries. Yep. So both, uh, Australia and ourselves have, even though we will be members of the facility- Mm-hmm.... uh, we've decided not to, uh, take grants because the purpose of the facility is to support- Yeah.

... Pacific. So obviously, the issue with that though is that it potentially impacts Tokelau's ability, which they could, if we were a member receiving grants, they could get it through that channel. Now the mechanism is we have to try and create rules that enable them to participate as a soc- Mm-hmm.... in a associate member- Mm-hmm.... which is unclear at this stage, whereas it would be certain if we had chosen to explore that and could have, of course, refused or, or not sought funding for any domestic aspects, but we will still have that

facility. Okay, on that? The... S- yeah. So, uh, based on, based on advice we received, the, the best approach for Tokelau was to, uh, in order to retain the right for them to receive benefits was to preserve that pathway for membership through the establishment agreement. Uh, because we had elected not to receive grants, it meant that Tokelau would be unable to receive grants.

So our interest was preserving that pathway through that............................ Okay. And you mentioned that the other nations will pay an annual membership fee. Mm-hmm. That's right. Uh- That seems to be at odds with the text that says, "The PRF shall not require payment of any amount to become, be or remain a PRF member."

So there's, uh, there is a membership fee requirement. Um, this reflects the, the sort of, uh, skin in the game, I guess, that, um, it was recognised that Pacific countries, uh, would, um, pay a small contribution in order to be able to receive benefits under the facility.

So how, how does that... Yeah. I mean, that seems quite a clear clause, but it's in, in the top- Yeah. ... top of page eight, clause D, "Shall not require payment of any amount to become- Yeah. Mm-hmm. "... be or remain a member." Yeah, so I think the part you're referring to is about, um, contribution towards the capitalisation. So it's not a structural requirement- Mm-hmm.

... that a Pacific country has to make a payment- (coughs)... towards capitalisation, but a membership fee is a separate, and that, that is required. Oh, okay. Where is that? Sorry. Just a minute. Uh, while you're finding that, can you just give us an overview of the, uh, likelihood of the 15 signatories?

Is it expected that all 15 signatories will become members? Yes. So, um, countries have different pathways for, uh, working through the ratification and approval process. Uh, some, uh, for some Pacific countries it's simpler than others, and we've seen that with, um, you know, the, the three countries that have, um, ratified so far. Um- Have any of them indicated that they intend to just participate as an observer as opposed to actually seeking membership?

No. All cap- All Pacific countries have signalled at this stage that they intend to ratify. Um, the Pacific Islands Forum is anticipating they will reach the eight countries, um, uh, in order for it to become into effect, uh, early, uh, next year. Um, but uh, yeah, some countries are just on a slower timeframe because of their domestic processes.

Um, so we- (coughs)... anticipate ratification will occur, um, throughout next year. So of the, um, all PIF nations, three didn't sign, being Somo- Samoa, and New Caledonia and French Polynesia. I understand New Caledonia and French Polynesia as French territories might have had to apply different processes, different mechanism.

Why didn't Samoa sign? Uh, Samoa would have been due to the elections- Yeah.... that were, um, occurring around, uh, around that time. Yes. So no one was able to sign on their behalf. Have they... Is there any indication of whether they tend to or have subsequent... I h- I haven't heard anything to suggest they, they won't, but we can follow that up and incorporate that in our, um, written, written response to that.

Thank you. So the 500 million target, we've already committed 20 million. Who else has made contributions and to what level? Australia is the largest contributor. Um, Australia has, uh, uh, announced a hundred million dollar, uh, Australian dollars contribution. Um, there's also, uh, a number of contributions coming in now from European countries. Um, it'd be fair to say that, uh, the 500 million target is relatively ambitious and uh, PIFS is, uh- (coughs) ... working with member countries to, um, fundraise for the, um, for the remaining

contributions to reach the 500 million. Um, over, over the next year. Uh, the US has, um, announced a contribution. Um, the contributions from European countries have been in the ranges of sort of three up to, up to 10 million. Um, the reality is- Sorry, what was the US one?

Uh, US contribution is 20 million US, US dollars. Um, although that's currently on pause due to the US, um, uh, review of, um, its aid funding. Okay.What's the anticipated cost to run the PRF annually? So, PIFs are still working through the financial modeling, um, both looking at, um, a range of scenarios for, uh, the allocation of grants and how that will be determined. For example, would that be based on

disaster risk or country size? And as part of that, are also doing some modeling around the administrative costs of uprunning the facility, um, in terms of, you know, staffing size and, um, whether they'll be satellite offices in other Pacific countries who are primarily domiciled in Tonga. So, th- those sorts of, um, models are still being worked through.

Okay. All right, very good. When's the next one? So, our Forum Leaders meeting? Yeah, um, it's in Palau. Um, I'm not sure of the actual dates yet. Is there likely to be some sort of investment committee or, or how is it, how are decisions going to be made around this capital fund, where it actually invests and who controls those decisions?

That's council involved there. Yeah. So, uh, that, those decisions still have to be made. There will be... Uh, in fact, one of the policies that sits under this establishment agreement is an investment policy. Uh, the expectation is that it'll be managed by professional, uh, fund managers. The Forum Secretariat has been talking to the World Bank and, and getting advice from their, um, sovereign funds management team.

Um, but it, it will be professionally managed. Okay. Very good. Thank you. Any further questions? No, that's good. Did you manage to find that one around the membership fee? Uh, I think we'll have to provide more information for you in our written submission. But the provision that you're talking about is referring to capitalization. Um, so when it says that it does not require payment of any amount to become a member, it's referring to not requiring a- Start-up.

Yeah. A start-up authorization. So, just to clarify, like, New Zealand's not looking, trying to become a member of the council or anything like that? So, we will be a member of the- We will be.... council. Like, yeah, but not the Board? Yeah. Okay.

Uh, no. So, board will be, um, uh... You know, those positions will be advertised, um, competitive selection- Selection. Um, yeah. Directors- Sorry, I just- That's all right. I just wanted to test that. Yeah. Yeah, so set up- Um, so the, the piece I was looking at was the top of page eight, sub-clause D, under the heading of finance and operations.

That's not capital. That's not a capital contribution. That's operation, isn't it? It's true. That requires a membership fee. Or cannot require a membership fee. Yeah, I guess there's also, um, clause C above that one, which says that the PRF Council may decide that a PRF member must make a financial contribution to the PRF.
Yes. So they may. Yeah. Yes. But then it follows that up by saying they cannot... Well, the next line-... r- r- require payment to be, remain or become... Yeah. I

think we'll have to follow that one up in our written submission. Yep, thank you. We appreciate that. Thank you. Yep. All right. Any further questions? Wonderful. Thanks for your time. Great. Thank you. Great. Thank you.
